text.title: Account Health Scoring Model Core Rules
text.summary: Five hard rules for a micro-agency account-health model with sub-20 clients.

section.title: The six signals + scoring
section.code:
S1 Retainer utilization variance over 4 weeks: |actual - bought| / bought ≤ 15% → 2 16-30% → 1 > 30% → 0
S2 Decision-maker engagement (calls + replies): ≥ 1 substantive touch per 2 weeks → 2 1 per 4 weeks → 1 < 1 per 4 weeks → 0
S3 Scope-creep delta vs. SOW (cumulative hours over scope): < 10% over OR signed-off CO → 2 10-25% over, no CO → 1 > 25% over, no CO → 0
S4 Payment punctuality (last 3 invoices avg DSO): ≤ net terms → 2 < 15 days late → 1 ≥ 15 days late OR open dispute → 0
S5 Referral / advocacy signal: gave referral OR public testimonial in last 6 months → 2 verbal positive in last 3 months → 1 none → 0
S6 Executive sponsor stability: same sponsor > 12 months → 2 same sponsor 3-12 months → 1 new sponsor in last 3 months → 0
Total → Green (10-12), Yellow (5-9), Red (0-4).

rule.statement: Health score uses ALL six signals. Single-signal scoring (NPS-only, utilization-only) hides correlated risks. A client at 100% utilization with a brand-new sponsor and 30-day late payment is yellow, not green.

rationale.source: Gainsight CS health-scoring data 2024; ChurnZero analyses

rule.rationale: Single-signal scoring produced 35-50% false-positive "healthy" classifications in Gainsight's 2024 retention study. Multi-signal aggregation reduced false positives to 8-12%. At sub-20 scale, each false positive costs a meaningful % of revenue.

rule.statement: Every signal is observable from data the agency already collects (timesheets, invoice records, calendar, Slack, CRM). "I feel like this client is unhappy" does not score. If a signal can't be measured, it's not in the model.
rule.rationale: Founder gut-feel scoring produces 30-50% week-on-week variance for the same account. Observable signals produce < 5%. The model exists to remove noise from health assessment so trends are real, not founder mood.

rule.statement: The 0/1/2 thresholds per signal are fixed at quarter start and not adjusted mid-quarter. Moving cutoffs to "make the dashboard look better" destroys the model. Cutoffs are reviewed at quarter-end retro, not weekly.

rationale.source: Statistical process control - SPC stability principle

rule.rationale: Moving thresholds is the classic Goodhart's Law failure: the metric becomes the goal, the goal stops measuring health. Stability lets trend lines be meaningful across weeks; quarter-end review catches genuine threshold staleness.

rule.statement: Score every account every week (Mondays). Skipping a week creates a perception gap that the model has nothing to say about (when in fact it has 0 changes to report — also valuable). Continuous cadence catches deterioration in 1-2 weeks vs. 6-8 weeks for quarterly-only review.

rationale.source: Customer-success industry cadence research; Gainsight 2024 best-practices

rule.rationale: Quarterly QBR alone catches problems at week 8-12; weekly health scoring catches them at week 1-2. The 4-6 week head start is the difference between save and churn.

rule.statement: Two trigger conditions force an account intervention: (a) score drops ≥ 2 points week-over-week, or (b) account sits Red for 2 consecutive weeks. Triggers produce a named action (call DM, scope-down conversation, internal post-mortem) within 48h.
rule.rationale: Without escalation triggers, the dashboard becomes wallpaper. Forcing a named action within 48h converts data into recovery. The 48h window aligns with research on intervention efficacy windows.

example.title: Account scored across 6 signals
example.code: Acme Inc., 2026-05-20: S1 utilization variance 12% → 2 S2 DM engagement weekly → 2 S3 scope creep 18% no CO → 1 S4 last 3 invoices avg 8d late → 1 S5 verbal positive Q1 → 1 S6 sponsor stable 14 months → 2 Total: 9 (Yellow, trending down from 11 last week) Trigger: 2-point drop → action: 30-min call with DM by Wed, raise scope CO.
example.note: Illustrates r1 (6 signals), r2 (observable), r5 (trigger fires).

example.title: NPS-only false-positive
example.code:
Acme Inc.: NPS = 9 (last quarterly survey) → "healthy"
In reality: utilization at 20% (paying for hours not used), DM hasn't replied in 3 weeks, 2 invoices 30 days late. NPS lags by quarter; cancellation arrives 8 weeks later as a surprise. Repair: score on the 6-signal model. Multi-signal would have flagged red at week 2.
example.note: Single-signal NPS health is the canonical false-positive pattern.
